summary: Michael Levin discusses his work on embodied minds, biological intelligence, and the spectrum of persuadability. He explores the limitations of physics in understanding life, the nature of cognition, and the potential for unconventional intelligences, including the idea of a Platonic space influencing physical reality.

main_entities.name: Persuadability
main_entities.context: The spectrum of how easily a system can be influenced, ranging from mechanical systems to intelligent beings.

main_entities.name: Cognitive Light Cone
main_entities.context: The scale of the largest goal that an agent can actively pursue.

main_entities.name: Xenobots
main_entities.context: Synthetic lifeforms created from frog cells, exhibiting novel behaviors and capabilities.

main_entities.name: Anthropods
main_entities.context: Synthetic lifeforms created from human cells, demonstrating self-motility and wound-healing abilities.

main_entities.name: Platonic Space
main_entities.context: A latent space of patterns and truths that influence physical reality and may contain different kinds of minds.

main_entities.name: TAME (Technological Approach to Mind Everywhere)
main_entities.context: A framework for understanding diverse bodies and minds through technological interventions and experiments.

main_entities.name: Algotype
main_entities.context: The type of algorithm a cell follows in a distributed sorting process.

main_entities.name: Mind-Brain Relationship
main_entities.context: The relationship between the mind and the brain, which Levin compares to the math-physics relationship.

main_entities.name: Cognitive Prosthetics
main_entities.context: Different ways of perceiving and interacting with the world.

main_entities.name: SUTI (Search for Unconventional Terrestrial Intelligences)
main_entities.context: The search for unconventional intelligences on Earth.

key_details.fact: Physics alone is insufficient to understand life and mind because it lacks the generative and creative capabilities needed to solve high-level problems.
There is no clear line between living and non-living things; instead, there is a continuum of agency and cognition.
The size of a system's cognitive light cone is correlated with its intelligence and agency.
Living things align their competent parts so that the collective has a larger cognitive light cone than the parts.
Anthrobots can roll back their age, being roughly 20% younger than the cells they come from.
Even simple sorting algorithms can exhibit unexpected competencies, such as delayed gratification and clustering.
The brain is a thin client interface to a Platonic space, where patterns and minds exist.
The mind-brain relationship is similar to the math-physics relationship, where non-physical truths impact physical objects.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Richard Watson
other_persons_mentioned.context: Coined the term 'mutual vulnerable knowing' in the context of bidirectional relationships with intelligent systems.

other_persons_mentioned.name: William James
other_persons_mentioned.context: Defined intelligence as achieving the same goal by different means.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Richard Dawkins
other_persons_mentioned.context: Mentioned in relation to memes and the idea of ideas as organisms.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Pythagoras
other_persons_mentioned.context: Discussed in relation to the Platonic space and the connection to mathematics.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Plato
other_persons_mentioned.context: Discussed in relation to the Platonic space and the connection to mathematics.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Penrose
other_persons_mentioned.context: Mentioned as a mathematician who believes in another space physically.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Donald Hoffman
other_persons_mentioned.context: Mentioned in relation to the idea of an interface to a world.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Anil Seth
other_persons_mentioned.context: Mentioned in relation to the idea that reality is a construct.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Jeremy Gay
other_persons_mentioned.context: Graphic artist who works with Michael Levin and drew figures for the TAME paper.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Kaining Zhang
other_persons_mentioned.context: Student who worked on the sorting algorithm project.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Adam Goldstein
other_persons_mentioned.context: Worked on the sorting algorithm project and named the property 'algotype'.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Steve Horvath
other_persons_mentioned.context: Developed the epigenetic clock used to measure the age of cells.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Corina Kofman
other_persons_mentioned.context: Co-authored a paper with Michael Levin on cases of humans with minimal brain tissue and normal IQ function.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Federico Pagosi
other_persons_mentioned.context: Member of Levin's group who worked on the causal emergence of chemical networks.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Bose
other_persons_mentioned.context: Studied how anesthesia affected animals, animal cells, and plants.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Joel Dietz
other_persons_mentioned.context: Gave a talk on radical Platonism and radical empiricism at the Platonic Space Conference.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Alexey Tolchinsky
other_persons_mentioned.context: Gave a talk on patterns and explanatory gaps in psychotherapy and whether God plays dice at the Platonic Space Conference.
